evals.prompt: Identify early product trends shaping the AI-assisted writing tools category in the next 12-24 months. We compete with ChatGPT, Claude, Grammarly, and specialized tools. What signals should we be monitoring?
evals.expected_output: Should identify multiple trend signals: (1) AI integration into existing workflows (not standalone) - Microsoft Copilot in Word, Google Duet in Workspace; trend toward embedded AI vs. separate tools; (2) Specialization - tools like GitHub Copilot, Cursor for code; Jasper for marketing copy; Clay for enrichment; trend away from general-purpose toward domain-specific; (3) Enterprise features becoming table stakes - permissions, SISO compliance, data security, audit logs; (4) Pricing consolidation - moving from per-user SaaS to per-enterprise licensing; (5) AI quality differentiation eroding - as base models become commoditized, differentiation shifts to UX, integrations, specialized training; (6) Regulation uncertainty - EU AI Act, potential guardrails on training data; impact on positioning; (7) User behavior shift - prompt engineering becoming skill; expectations of AI getting smarter; tolerance for errors decreasing. Should recommend monitoring: Gartner Magic Quadrant movements, feature releases from major players, pricing changes, EU regulation developments, new startup announcements in specialized categories.

evals.prompt: What weak signals could disrupt our current B2B SaaS product strategy? Our product is in project management, focused on mid-market teams (10-100 people). We've been iterating on existing features for 3 years.
evals.expected_output: Should identify potential disruptive weak signals: (1) AI agents could automate tasks without UI - users describe what they want, AI does it; threatens traditional project management UIs; (2) No-code automation platforms (Zapier, Make) enabling teams to build custom workflows faster than software development; threatens our positioning as 'do everything'; (3) Vertical-specific tools gaining traction - teams choosing specialized tools for their industry/workflow rather than horizontal platform; (4) Asynchronous-first tools (Slack for work chats, Threads for async reviews) fragmenting what used to be centralized in one platform; (5) Open-source alternatives (Plane, Taiga) gaining enterprise adoption; (6) Unbundling trend - teams using best-of-breed for each function rather than all-in-one; threatens our integration moat. Should recommend: (a) Monitor product launches from AI-first companies entering project management; (b) Watch if customers start using multiple tools instead of consolidating; (c) Track vertical SaaS adoption in your target markets; (d) Evaluate AI agent feasibility for your domain. Risk: 3 years of feature iteration without major differentiation could make you vulnerable to new paradigm.

evals.prompt: Create a trend landscape map for the health/wellness app category. Identify 5-6 major trends and categorize by adoption stage (emerging, growing, mature) and business impact (low, medium, high).
evals.expected_output: Should create map with trends and positioning: High Impact & Mature: (1) Wearable integration (Apple Watch, Fitbit sync) - table stakes for fitness apps; (2) Subscription models - LTV driver, but also table stakes. High Impact & Emerging: (3) AI-powered personalization - tailored workout/nutrition recommendations; (4) Mental health + physical health bundling - holistic wellness; (5) Community and social features - accountability, motivation; (6) Continuous monitoring without active input (ambient wellness) - sensors, passive data. Medium Impact & Growing: (7) Telehealth integration - connecting apps to doctors; (8) Corporate wellness programs - B2B2C model; (9) Genetic/biometric data - personalization at deeper level. Should chart on 2x2 with bubble sizes representing adoption velocity. Should note: Top-right (high impact + mature) = table stakes; top-left (high impact + emerging) = where competitive differentiation is; bottom-left = nice-to-have; bottom-right = avoid.

evals.prompt: Compare emerging UX patterns across fintech onboarding flows. We're redesigning our onboarding for a banking app. What patterns are competitors using? What's becoming table stakes vs. innovation?
evals.expected_output: Should research and identify patterns: Table Stakes (seen across Revolut, Mercury, Chime, Square): (1) 2-3 minute signup (mobile-first, minimal fields); (2) Instant account activation (not waiting for verification); (3) Biometric auth (fingerprint/face); (4) In-app verification of identity (real-time AI/document checks); (5) Debit card instant issuance (virtual card available immediately). Emerging Innovations: (1) Personality quiz at start ('What's your financial goal?') to tailor experience - personalized onboarding; (2) Gamification (point milestones, celebration moments) to drive engagement; (3) Social proof (show friends on platform, referral incentives immediately); (4) Progressive disclosure (don't overwhelm with all features; reveal as user explores); (5) AI-powered recommendations based on behavior patterns observed during early usage. Should recommend: Table stakes features are non-negotiable; focus innovation on personalization/progressive disclosure. Should note: Speed is now expected; complexity is the blocker. Every additional step = drop-off.

evals.prompt: Translate this cultural shift into product opportunities: Gen Z consumers increasingly prefer smaller, niche communities over mega-platforms. They're leaving Instagram/TikTok in favor of Discord, BeReal, private Snapchat groups, subreddits.
evals.expected_output: Should identify product implications: (1) Shift from public to private/community-based - opportunities in private social, community platforms, paid communities; (2) Authenticity over curation - BeReal, Snapchat appeal because 'realness,' not polished; threat to Instagram's positioning; (3) Niche identity - communities around specific interests vs. follows/followers; (4) Community moderation matters - Discord appeals because mods keep community healthy; Instagram struggling with toxicity; (5) Ownership/control - preference for platforms where creators own their audience and data (private communities vs. algorithmic feeds). Product opportunities: (a) For social platforms - build community features, moderator tools, private group discovery; (b) For community tools - emphasize authenticity, privacy, ownership, creator control; (c) For existing large platforms - risk of becoming 'uncool' unless you dramatically increase community features and reduce algorithmic feed dominance. Should recommend: If you're a large platform, this is existential risk; if you're building new, lean into niche + community + authenticity positioning.

evals.prompt: Assess which of these current trends are hype vs. likely to see broad adoption: (a) Crypto for everyday payments, (b) Voice-first interfaces, (c) Vertical video on desktops, (d) AI-assisted search (perplexity, etc.), (e) Subscription fatigue as service model shift.
evals.expected_output: Should assess each: (a) Crypto for everyday payments: HYPE - Stuck at innovators/early adopters since 2020; no mainstream adoption; regulatory headwinds; lack of killer use case over credit cards. Unlikely to mainstream. (b) Voice-first interfaces: PARTIAL - Growing in automotive (Apple Carplay, Android Auto) and smart speakers (Alexa, Google Home), but desktop/mobile still prefer touch. Likely to grow in specific contexts (driving, hands-free) but not replace UI everywhere. (c) Vertical video on desktops: HYPE - Gen Z prefers it, but hardware constraints (monitors are horizontal); unlikely to replace horizontal video on desktop despite TikTok's success on mobile. (d) AI-assisted search: LIKELY - Already seeing adoption with Perplexity, ChatGPT, Claude; users find it more conversational and direct than Google; likely to mainstream as younger cohorts adopt; already table stakes for knowledge workers. (e) Subscription fatigue: LIKELY - Real behavioral trend; consumers actively cutting subscriptions; pushback on price increases; drives shift to ad-supported, tiered pricing, bundling strategies. Should note: Assess on Rogers curve (innovators → mainstream), actual user adoption (not just hype articles), regulatory/technical headwinds.

evals.prompt: Our competitor just launched a major AI feature into their product. Should we panic and rush to match it, or take a strategic wait-and-see? How do we decide?
evals.expected_output: Should recommend decision framework: (1) Assess adoption likelihood: Is this solving a real customer pain or is it hype? Monitor customer response for 4-6 weeks before committing resources. (2) Assess customer demand: Are your customers asking for this feature? Or did the competitor create demand with marketing? (3) Assess timing: Is this first-mover advantage (must be early) or table stakes in 12-18 months (can afford to wait and do better)? (4) Assess your capability: Can you build this better/cheaper than they did? Or will you be playing catch-up? (5) Assess roadmap alignment: Does this fit your vision, or are you chasing competitor moves? Strategic risk of reactive product strategy. Decision: (a) If customers are demanding + competitor showing strong adoption = rush to match (don't want to lose deals); (b) If hype but no customer demand = monitor and wait; plan to build in 6-12 months when feature is proven; (c) If you can differentiate = build it your way (don't copy; leapfrog); (d) If timing is before your roadmap = reassess priorities, but don't panic. Most important: Understand your customers' needs first; use competitor as signal but don't let it drive strategy.

evals.prompt: Create a scenario plan for how AI-powered task automation trends might evolve over next 24 months and what that means for our project management tool. Consider: optimistic, base case, and pessimistic scenarios.
evals.expected_output: Should develop 3 scenarios: OPTIMISTIC: AI agents can reliably automate 60%+ of task management work (scheduling, task prioritization, meeting notes, etc.) within 18 months. Customers move from 'use PM tool for all work' to 'AI handles routine tasks, tool provides oversight.' Implication: Our value shifts to orchestration + visibility layer; need to partner with AI agents or build AI-first; risk of commoditization. Action: Start AI partnerships, invest in API openness, build as integration hub. BASE CASE: AI handles some routine tasks but users still prefer human control for complex work. Adoption is 30-40% slower than optimistic; customers still use PM tools but expect AI assistance. Implication: AI becomes table stakes feature; we need to build it but competition is fierce. Action: Build AI features into product roadmap next 6-12 months; focus on quality over speed. PESSIMISTIC: AI for task automation is overhyped; users find current AI unreliable and errors are costly; adoption stalls below 10%. Customers stay in traditional PM workflows. Implication: No immediate threat; our current product positioning is safe. Action: Ignore AI trends in PM; focus on improving existing features. Should recommend: MOST LIKELY = Base Case. Prepare for AI becoming table stakes while monitoring Optimistic/Pessimistic signals. Build flexibility into roadmap; don't bet company on any single scenario.
